sanskrit_devanagari:
एवं परम्पराप्राप्तमिमं राजर्षयो विदुः ।
स कालेनेह महता योगो नष्टः परन्तप ॥ २ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
evaṁ paramparā-prāptam imaṁ rājarṣayo viduḥ |
sa kāleneha mahatā yogo naṣṭaḥ paran-tapa || 2 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: This supreme science was thus received through the chain of disciplic succession, and the saintly kings understood it in that way. But in course of time the succession was broken, and therefore the science as it is appears to be lost.

This supreme science was thus received through the chain of disciplic succession, and the saintly kings understood it in that way. But in course of time the succession was broken, and therefore the science as it is appears to be lost.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1535 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: It is clearly stated that the Gītā was especially meant for the saintly kings because they were to execute its purpose in ruling over the citizens. Certainly Bhagavad-gītā was never meant for the demonic persons, who would dissipate its value for no one’s benefit and would devise all types of interp…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.2 Rajarsayah, the king-sages, those who were kings and sages (at the same time); viduh, knew; imam, this Yoga; which was evam parampara-praptam, received thus through a regular succession of Ksatriyas.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-2-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads naṣṭaḥ as the yoga having become disrupted because the strength of those receiving it had weakened — those without self-control received it, leading to its decline. The yoga itself is not destroyed in essence; the chain of competent receivers broke.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.1 – 4.2 The Lord said — This Karma Yoga declared to you should not be considered as having been taught now merely, for creating encouragement in you for war. I Myself had taught this Yoga to Vivasvan at the commencement of Manu’s age as a means for all beings to attain release, which is man’s supr…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-2-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as continuous with 4.1: this yoga was passed through royal sages who knew it, but in the long course of time it was lost — meaning unrealised, no longer practiced as the path to liberation it was given as. The verse establishes the necessity of Krishna's re-instruction.

positions.rendering: lost in the sense that competent receivers (those with self-control) became scarce
lost in the sense of unrealised — the yoga as a path to liberation ceased to be lived

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three agree the yoga was 'lost' but locate the loss differently. Prabhupada flattens to a broken transmission chain. Shankara emphasises the receiver-side — when those receiving the yoga lacked self-control, the yoga's transmission failed. Ramanuja emphasises the praxis-side — the yoga ceased to be lived as a soteriological path. The Advaita reading is more focused on epistemic competence; the Vishishtadvaita reading is more focused on lived practice.

literal_meaning: Thus received in lineage, the royal sages knew this yoga; over a long time, Arjuna, that yoga was lost in this world.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The lineage of paramparā is acknowledged: royal sages preserved the yoga across generations. But Krishna names a fact that bears doctrinal weight — the yoga was lost (naṣṭaḥ) over time. This sets up 4.3, where Krishna explains why he is re-imparting it now. The verse honors the principle that even authentic transmission decays without sustained practice and worthy successors.
